utterances: You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And let's get--John, by the way, thanks for the call.
We appreciate it.
And let's see if we can get another caller on the line.
This is Bill.
Bill's with us from San Diego.
Yes, hi.
Hi.
I work at Scripps Institution of Oceanography, and one of the things we do is we do deploy buoys to do research for--you know, to add more information to improve the data prediction models.
And I've actually--you know, I haven't spoken with ...(unintelligible) before Mr. Black, but I've seen his name around.

And one of the things that we've--you know, we encounter is that, you know, the hurricane tracking predictions is pretty good and the hurricane intensities is--even in the best of times when all the equipment's working and all the--you know, there's no malfunctions.
It's a difficult problem.
It's not--the basic research hasn't been done to allow for a real accurate prediction of the intensity.
And...
And, you know, one of the problems that they run into is that it's difficult to get data.
The storms--like, for example, we put drifting buoys in the water.
And to get them in front of a hurricane, we air deploy them with the Air Force.
And to put 20 buoys in the water costs us about $300,000, to build the buoys, get them on a plane, rig them with parachutes and put them.
And so that's 20 data points and you have a storm that's, you know, hundreds of miles across.
It's not a lot of information.
So it's expensive to get data.
It's a big ocean, you know.
So that's just one of the...
Mike Black, do these difficulties sound familiar?
Oh, very much so.
And, Bill, I think we may have actually met at some time.
I'm very familiar with the work there at Scripps.

In fact, we have partnered with Scripps on this very thing he's talking about, of deploying ocean and atmospheric probes, having the Air Force do that as a service for the research community at large, both NOAA and the Office of Naval Research, as well as institutions like Scripps.
And he's right.
It is a very expensive proposition, but it's invaluable.

And he's also correct in stating that the hurricane problem, the prediction, the predictability of hurricanes, is a very complex problem that involves not only all scales within the atmosphere, but involves directly knowing what the ocean conditions are like, and not just at the surface but beneath the surface as well.

And there's been a lot of emphasis in the last several years--not only by NOAA, but by university and other governmental agencies--into the ocean portion of the problem, and there's been some advances made but not nearly enough.

I think The Herald pointed out correctly that we at the Hurricane Research Division are using donated devices from the Navy that are 20 years old but are not supposed to be working but, in fact, still do, and releasing them from our NOAA aircraft to measure the upper ocean.
And we'd love to be able to get that data in real time to the forecasters, and we simply don't have the financial support to do so.
Bill, thanks very much for the call.
We appreciate it.
You're welcome.
Thank you.
And, Mike Black, before we let you go, I do have to ask you a question.
You work at NOAA.
NOAA representatives, again, declined to join us today, but why are you talking to The Miami Herald and to us?
Aren't you afraid of losing your job?
I hope NOAA, you know, wouldn't consider that as option.

I'm able to speak to you primarily because not only am I a public servant, first and foremost, as a meteorologist with the Hurricane Research Division at NOAA, but I'm also union steward of the National Weather Service Employees Organization here in Miami.
And that allows me the freedom--it's a right and a freedom--to speak with press, to speak with other government officials where other non-union members may not be able to do so.
And I felt that this was an important enough issue--I've been doing this for 20 years.
I feel the work that we do--and I mean NOAA as a whole; I'm very proud to be a NOAA employee--is valuable to the country and that these problems need to be brought up so that they can be corrected.
Mike Black, thanks very much for being with us today.
Thank you.
And Kyrgyzstan has assured the United States that it can keep its vital air base in the former Soviet state.
The pledged was formalized today after negotiations between Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice and Kyrgyzstan President Kurmanbek Bakiev.
And you can hear details on those stories and, of course, much more later today on ""All Things Considered"" from NPR News.
Tomorrow on TALK OF THE NATION, what does it really take to launch a major relief effort?
It's not just the cash.
Nature has to be overcome.
Politics get in the way.
We'll take you inside the process tomorrow on TALK OF THE NATION.
Today, we're talking about how to predict hurricanes and whether we can do it better.
Still with us is Debbie Cenziper, a reporter at The Miami Herald.
If you've joined us late, we're talking about that newspaper's eight-month-long investigation into every hurricane that's made landfall since Hurricane Andrew 13 years ago.
If you're interested, you can still join us as we now switch to the politics of this question.
You can e-mail us: totn@npr.org.
And, Debbie, what's the reaction in Congress to the complaints from the Hurricane Center that it's been underfunded?
Well, I think that's kind of an interesting point you raise.
These problems have been going on for years, but not a lot--this hasn't really bubbled up very often to Congress.

I interviewed every Hurricane Center director going back to Robert Simpson in the 1970s, and they all talk about, you know, a culture of fear within the agency, that they've been told they're really not allowed to be talking publicly about these things.
They're not, of course, allowed to lobby Congress and, you know, they've talked about gaps in research and gaps in equipment kind of privately for a long time, but not a lot has been said publicly.
I can imagine that there's scarcely a federal agency anywhere that does not talk about gaps in funding at this point.
Yeah.
I understand what you're saying, and I think that that's a good point.
But it's not just a question of funding; it's a question of priorities.
And the other issue is it's not going to take a lot of money to fix this problem.
That's what people in the Hurricane Center and at Weather Service field offices talk about, is if you really add it all up, it's not a very expensive problem to fix.
Let's get another caller on the line.
This is Lawrence, Lawrence calling us from Philadelphia.
Good afternoon.
How are you?
Very well, thank you.
My question has to do with one particular legislator's take on the National Weather Service and the Hurricane Center, and that legislator is our Pennsylvania senator, Rick Santorum.
He's a very powerful senator.

And he a couple of months ago proposed SA-13, which is a bill which would prevent the National Weather Service from disseminating its information for free to the public wherever there was a private company that wanted to disseminate that.
And here in Pennsylvania, the biggest private company in that business is AccuWeather of State College, Pennsylvania.
I can understand Senator Santorum's wanting to protect and enhance jobs at AccuWeather, but I personally have relatives in Florida and use the National Hurricane Center's Web site quite frequently.
It provides a number of prediction tools and graphics that have been very, very helpful to me.

And I wondered if your reporter had any information about the ways in which the National Hurricane Center would be affected if its outlet for information to the public were to be shut down by Senator Santorum's bill.
Debbie Cenziper.
Yeah, I'm very aware of that bill, and we tried to stay away from that a little bit in this project.
One, because I don't think the bill has all that--has generated much support in Congress.
And, you know, it's real controversial.
I mean, Senator Bill Nelson in Florida is absolutely opposed to it.
People are worried that, you know, basically they'd be paying for weather predictions that they can now get for free from the Weather Service.
So we tried to stay away from that.
We focused mostly on research, equipment, support from Washington.
Lawrence, thanks for the call.
Thank you very much.
And let's see if we can talk with Mariel, excuse me.
Mariel's calling from Cambridge, Massachusetts.
Yes.

I think it's really important that the American public have a decent understanding of the procedures for funding the hurricane centers, the people who do the work and the equipment that needs to be used, because if it's not evaluated by scientists, then the decisions for funding are going to be made by hacks and politicians.
I just think it's critically important that the American people understand the process for getting funding for these very vital agencies.
Well, how does the process work, Debbie?
You know, it's kind of a gray area.
I mean, we pointed out in Monday's--yesterday's Miami Herald that researchers have kind of been asking informally for years for more money.
But you know, Congress doesn't necessarily know that because the research or the funding requests kind of have to bubble up through the agency.
And so nobody really knows publicly how much, say, the Hurricane Research Division has been asking for for years.
NOAA kind of makes the call on what they want to submit to the president and to Congress, and it's kind of hard to track how, you know--what the agencies are actually asking for.
What agencies are they bubbling up through?
They bubble up--well, I mean, they bubble up through NOAA.
That's kind of the umbrella agency.
And NOAA--officials within NOAA make the call on what budgets they want to support and, you know, what they want to request, what projects they want to...
And what again is the definition for NOAA?
The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
Well, I don't understand why they would not want this money to be allocated that's necessary for the safety of the American public.
Yeah, that's what their own forecasters say--are saying the exact same thing.
Yeah.
Yeah.
In fact, one point that we made in Sunday's Herald is that administrative costs for NOAA just in the last year have grown tremendously.
And the Weather Service, which oversees the Hurricane Center and forecasting offices all over the country, had to cut almost $40 million from its budget.
And that meant, you know, equipment would not be repaired as quickly.
Training for forecasters--a lot of that was cut while NOAA's overhead basically grew.
So, yeah, it's...
I mean, it sounds like scientists aren't the most important people there; it's really a bureaucracy that lives to feed itself.
Mariel, thanks very much for the call.
Thank you.
We appreciate it.
And, Debbie Cenziper, if people were interested in reading this series, they're on the Web site at The Miami Herald?
Yeah, Herald.com.
It's--yeah, every day it's posted on the Web site.
Debbie Cenziper, thanks very much, and good luck with the series.
Thanks so much.
